The distribution of land continues with methodical precision. Simeon receives cities within Judah's territory because Judah's portion proved too large—a practical adjustment that required no complaint or protest. The detailed lists that follow—Zebulun, Issachar, Asher, Naphtali, and Dan—are not meant to be merely ornamental. Each name represents a real place where real families would plant their roots, build their homes, and worship their God. The specificity matters. God is not content with vague promises. He gives His people actual land, actual cities, actual inheritance. The boundaries are named. The cities are named. The villages are named. This is the God who deals with particulars, not abstractions.

Yet notice what interrupts the monotony of city lists: Dan's story breaks through in verse 47. The Danites lost their territory. What was given had to be retaken by force. Leshem fell to their sword, and they claimed it instead, renaming it after their father. This is not a footnote. This is a warning embedded in the narrative. Not every tribe would possess their land easily or keep it permanently. The gift of inheritance came with the responsibility to secure it, and failure came swiftly to those who did not. The land was given, but it still had to be fought for, held, and guarded. God's grace does not eliminate struggle; it frames it with purpose.

The chapter concludes with an act of justice that is almost easy to miss. Joshua, who led Israel through forty years of wilderness and conquest, receives his own inheritance at last—Timnath-serah in the hill country of Ephraim. The people themselves give it to him, as the Lord commanded. This is not self-seeking. This is not Joshua taking what he wants. He receives what the Lord appoints through the people He led. He rebuilds the city and settles there. The great leader does not live in a palace separate from his people. He lives in the land like everyone else, subject to the same conditions, the same boundaries, the same God.

The division is complete. Eleazar the priest, Joshua, and the family heads distributed the land by lot at Shiloh before the Lord. The structure is remarkable: priest, judge, and the elders together, acting before God, using the lot to strip human preference from the process. What emerges is not what any man wanted but what God appointed. The land is divided. The promise is kept. But the real test—whether Israel would actually remain faithful in the land, whether they would honor God in the cities now named as their own—lies ahead. The inheritance has been distributed. Now it must be lived in faithfully.
